title: Blackpool fact of the day, observations on northern England - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: A short MR link-post quoting Chris Moss's book on Lancashire, noting Blackpool Central's rise and fall as a rail hub and contrasting the cultural temperaments of Lancashire and Yorkshire.

themes.theme: Rise and fall of northern rail

insights.claim: Blackpool Central was the world's busiest railway station in 1911 and later lost the most platforms of any UK station in the 1964 Beeching cuts.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A vivid case of infrastructure boom-then-abandonment tied to changing leisure and transport demand — a reminder of how durable assets can be stranded.
insights.quote: Blackpool Central was the world’s busiest station in 1911.

insights.claim: Chris Moss characterizes Lancashire as Irish, Atlantic, Catholic and forward-looking versus Yorkshire as European, North Sea-facing, Anglican and 'past tense.'
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A subjective cultural-geography framing of how neighboring English regions differ in orientation and temperament.
insights.locator: I’ve never felt or fully understood the alleged tension
